summary: The Isaiah textual traditions exhibit extensive theological revision, particularly in the LXX and Targum. The LXX demonstrates systematic eschatological sharpening and universalizing tendencies, reinterpreting prophetic oracles for Hellenistic-era audiences. The Targum shows aggressive messianic heightening, transforming ambiguous oracles into explicit references to the Messiah, alongside proto-rabbinic interpretive glosses that align the text with emerging halakhic concerns.
summary_plain: Translators of Isaiah made significant theological changes to the original Hebrew. The Greek version intensified prophecies about the end times and God's universal reign. The Aramaic version added explicit references to the Messiah where the Hebrew was ambiguous, and inserted interpretive explanations that reflected early rabbinic traditions.

revisions.mt_reading: הָעַלְמָה (ha-almah, 'the young woman') shall conceive and bear a son
revisions.revised_reading: ἡ παρθένος (hē parthenos, 'the virgin') shall conceive and bear a son
revisions.evidence: The LXX substitutes 'parthenos' (virgin) for the Hebrew 'almah' (young woman of marriageable age), a lexical choice that intensifies the miraculous character of the sign. While 'almah' denotes a young woman without explicit reference to virginity, 'parthenos' in Hellenistic Greek normally indicates virginal status. This shift transforms an originally ambiguous dynastic oracle into a text amenable to heightened messianic—and eventually Christian—interpretation. The translator's choice cannot be explained by lexical equivalence alone, as 'neanis' would have been a more neutral rendering.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek translator chose a word meaning 'virgin' instead of the Hebrew word meaning 'young woman,' making the prophecy seem more miraculous and easier to interpret as pointing to a special messianic figure.
scholarly_citations: van der Kooij, The Oracle of Tyre, pp. 89-92
Seeligmann, The Septuagint Version of Isaiah, pp. 98-101
revisions.counter_arguments: Some argue 'parthenos' was simply the standard Greek equivalent for 'almah' in the translator's Vorlage tradition, and that messianic interpretation is a post-translation development rather than a translation motivation.

revisions.mt_reading: For a child is born to us, a son is given to us, and the government shall be upon his shoulder, and his name shall be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace
revisions.revised_reading: For a child is born to us, a son is given to us, and he shall receive the Torah upon him to keep it, and his name shall be called from before the Wonderful Counselor, the Mighty God, the Everlasting One: Messiah (משיחא), in whose days peace shall increase upon us
revisions.evidence: The Targum restructures the verse to explicitly identify the figure as 'Messiah' (משיחא), removing ambiguity about the referent. The divine titles are reattributed to God rather than the child ('called from before the Wonderful Counselor'), reflecting proto-rabbinic discomfort with attributing divine names directly to a human figure. The insertion of Torah-obedience language ('receive the Torah upon him to keep it') aligns the messianic figure with rabbinic ideals of halakhic observance. This represents interpretive translation, not textual corruption.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translation explicitly calls this figure 'Messiah' and clarifies that the divine names belong to God, not the child. It also adds that the Messiah will keep the Torah, reflecting Jewish interpretive traditions.
scholarly_citations: Chilton, The Isaiah Targum, pp. 19-21
Smelik, The Targum of Judges, pp. 78-80
revisions.counter_arguments: The explicit messianic reading may reflect the plain sense of the Hebrew as understood in the targumist's context, rather than an innovation; the royal ideology of the Hebrew may have already implied messianic categories.

revisions.mt_reading: Behold my servant (עַבְדִּי), whom I uphold, my chosen, in whom my soul delights; I have put my spirit upon him, he will bring forth justice to the nations
revisions.revised_reading: Jacob is my servant (παῖς), I will help him; Israel is my chosen one, my soul has accepted him; I have put my spirit upon him, he will bring forth judgment to the nations
revisions.evidence: The LXX explicitly identifies the Servant as 'Jacob' and 'Israel,' removing the ambiguity of the MT's indefinite referent. This reflects a collective eschatological interpretation: the Servant is identified with the restored people of Israel who will exercise judgment among the nations. The shift from 'justice' (משפט as forensic norm) to 'judgment' (κρίσις as eschatological verdict) intensifies the future orientation. This interpretive move aligns with Schaper's thesis that the LXX systematically reorients prophetic texts toward eschatological fulfillment through the agency of a restored Israel.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek version names the Servant as 'Jacob' and 'Israel,' clarifying that this prophecy refers to the whole people of Israel who will one day judge the nations, not just an individual figure.
scholarly_citations: Schaper, Eschatology in the Greek Psalter, pp. 54-56
Seeligmann, The Septuagint Version of Isaiah, pp. 45-47
revisions.counter_arguments: The identification with Jacob/Israel may reflect a Hebrew Vorlage that already contained these glosses, or represent an attempt at literal precision rather than theological innovation.

revisions.mt_reading: He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief... he was wounded for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities
revisions.revised_reading: Behold, my servant the Messiah shall prosper... Then the glory of all kingdoms shall be despised and cease; they shall be infirm and mourning, as a man of pains... He will build the sanctuary that was profaned for our sins
revisions.evidence: The Targum radically restructures the Suffering Servant passage, applying suffering language to Israel's enemies rather than to the Servant-Messiah himself. The Messiah is glorified and victorious; the suffering is transferred to the nations or to Israel's sins abstractly. The phrase 'my servant the Messiah' (עבדי משיחא) is explicitly inserted in 52:13. The phrase 'he will build the sanctuary' transforms the atoning function from vicarious suffering to cultic restoration. This represents a proto-rabbinic rejection of a suffering messianic figure, consistent with later rabbinic discomfort with Christian appropriation of Isaiah 53.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translation explicitly identifies the Servant as the Messiah but removes all references to the Messiah himself suffering. Instead, it says Israel's enemies will suffer, and the Messiah will rebuild the Temple. This reflects Jewish resistance to the idea of a suffering Messiah.
scholarly_citations: Chilton, The Glory of Israel, pp. 86-95
Levey, The Messiah: An Aramaic Interpretation, pp. 63-69
revisions.counter_arguments: The targumic reading may preserve an ancient interpretation that the Servant represents the righteous remnant of Israel, not necessarily a later anti-Christian polemic; the collective interpretation has independent attestation.

revisions.mt_reading: I saw the Lord (אֲדֹנָי) sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up
revisions.revised_reading: I saw the glory of the Lord (יקרא דיי) resting upon a throne, high and lifted up
revisions.evidence: The Targum substitutes 'the glory of the Lord' (יקרא דיי) for the direct vision of 'the Lord' (אדני), inserting the Shekinah-theology buffer between the prophet and the divine essence. This reflects proto-rabbinic reluctance to affirm unmediated anthropomorphic visions of God. The substitution of 'glory' for the divine name is systematic throughout Isaiah's throne vision (6:1-5), consistent with targumic practice elsewhere. The theological motivation is transparent: to protect divine transcendence while preserving the visionary experience.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic version changes 'I saw the Lord' to 'I saw the glory of the Lord,' avoiding the suggestion that the prophet saw God directly. This reflects early Jewish discomfort with anthropomorphic descriptions of God.
scholarly_citations: Chilton, The Isaiah Targum, pp. 14-15
Muñoz León, Gloria de la Shekina, pp. 112-118
revisions.counter_arguments: The 'glory' language may simply explicate what was already implicit in the Hebrew concept of God's kabod; the change may be exegetical clarification rather than theological correction.

revisions.mt_reading: In that day there will be five cities in the land of Egypt that speak the language of Canaan and swear allegiance to the LORD of hosts. One of these will be called the City of Destruction (עיר ההרס)
revisions.revised_reading: In that day there will be five cities in the land of Egypt speaking the language of Canaan and swearing by the name of the Lord Sabaoth. One shall be called 'City of Righteousness' (πόλις ἀσεδεκ)
revisions.evidence: The LXX substitutes 'City of Righteousness' (πόλις ἀσεδεκ, likely rendering עיר הצדק) for MT's 'City of Destruction' (עיר ההרס). Some Hebrew manuscripts read עיר החרס ('City of the Sun,' a reference to Heliopolis). The LXX's positive reading removes the ambiguity and potential negativity associated with 'destruction,' harmonizing with the oracle's overall positive tone regarding Egypt's conversion. This may reflect either a variant Vorlage or a deliberate theological softening to eliminate incongruity in an oracle of salvation.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek version changes 'City of Destruction' to 'City of Righteousness,' making the prophecy about Egypt entirely positive and removing a confusing or negative reference.
scholarly_citations: van der Kooij, The Oracle of Tyre, pp. 56-58
Seeligmann, The Septuagint Version of Isaiah, pp. 76-78
revisions.counter_arguments: The LXX may translate a genuine Hebrew variant (עיר החרס or עיר הצדק) rather than theologically revising the MT; textual pluriformity in the Second Temple period makes this difficult to adjudicate.

revisions.mt_reading: In all their distress, he was distressed (לֹא צָר), and the angel of his presence saved them
revisions.revised_reading: Not an ambassador (οὐ πρέσβυς), nor a messenger (ἄγγελος), but he himself (αὐτὸς κύριος) saved them
revisions.evidence: The MT's לֹא צָר is notoriously difficult; if read as 'he was distressed,' it attributes suffering to God. The LXX resolves this by reading לא צר as 'not an envoy' (deriving צר from a root meaning 'narrow' or 'ambassador'), and restructures the syntax to emphasize God's direct saving action without angelic intermediation. This removes divine pathos ('distressed') and simultaneously elevates God's direct agency over angelic mediation, consistent with LXX tendencies to protect divine transcendence while paradoxically emphasizing divine immanence in salvific action.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Hebrew text may say 'God was distressed,' which attributes human emotion to God. The Greek version reinterprets the text to say God saved Israel directly, not through angels, avoiding the emotional attribution.
scholarly_citations: Seeligmann, The Septuagint Version of Isaiah, pp. 28-30
Troxel, LXX-Isaiah as Translation and Interpretation, pp. 145-148
revisions.counter_arguments: The LXX may simply reflect a different Hebrew Vorlage or a legitimate alternative parsing of a grammatically ambiguous text; the anthropomorphic reading may not have been available to the Greek translator.

revisions.mt_reading: A shoot shall come out from the stump of Jesse, and a branch from his roots shall bear fruit. The spirit of the LORD shall rest upon him
revisions.revised_reading: A king shall come forth from the sons of Jesse, and the Messiah (משיחא) from his sons' sons shall be exalted. And a spirit from before the LORD shall rest upon him
revisions.evidence: The Targum explicitly identifies the Davidic figure as 'the Messiah' (משיחא), removing all ambiguity about the referent. The organic imagery ('shoot,' 'branch') is replaced with dynastic-political language ('king,' 'sons'). The phrase 'spirit from before the LORD' (רוח מן קדם יי) employs the 'from before' formula to mediate divine presence, consistent with proto-rabbinic circumlocution. This represents a double move: messianic heightening through explicit titular identification and anthropomorphism avoidance through prepositional distancing.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic version explicitly calls the Davidic descendant 'the Messiah' and changes poetic plant imagery to direct royal language, making the messianic interpretation unmistakable.
scholarly_citations: Chilton, The Isaiah Targum, pp. 24-26
Cathcart and Gordon, The Targum of the Minor Prophets, pp. 34-36
revisions.counter_arguments: The royal-messianic interpretation may simply make explicit what was already the plain sense of the Hebrew in its original context; the Targum may preserve rather than innovate the intended reading.

revisions.mt_reading: A voice cries: 'In the wilderness prepare the way of the LORD, make straight in the desert a highway for our God'
revisions.revised_reading: A voice of one crying in the wilderness: 'Prepare the way of the Lord, make straight the paths of our God'
revisions.evidence: The LXX restructures the syntax so that the voice cries 'in the wilderness' rather than calling for preparation 'in the wilderness.' This syntactic shift, while subtle, intensifies the eschatological drama by locating the prophetic voice in the liminal space of the wilderness, a traditional locus of divine encounter and new exodus. The change from 'highway' (מְסִלָּה) to 'paths' (τρίβους) pluralizes and generalizes the imagery, suggesting multiple routes of return, consistent with diaspora concerns. This reading was influential for the NT's appropriation (all four Gospels cite this version).
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek version changes the sentence structure so that the prophet is in the wilderness, not that the road should be built in the wilderness. This makes the prophecy more dramatic and influenced how early Christians read this text.
scholarly_citations: Schaper, Eschatology in the Greek Psalter, pp. 112-115
Watts, Isaiah 1-33, pp. 78-80
revisions.counter_arguments: The syntactic difference may be a function of Greek word order conventions rather than theological motivation; the translator may simply be following Hellenistic Greek syntax norms.

revisions.mt_reading: Yet it was the will of the LORD to crush him; he has put him to grief (הֶחֱלִי)
revisions.revised_reading: And the Lord wishes to cleanse him from his blow (καθαρίσαι αὐτὸν τῆς πληγῆς)
revisions.evidence: The LXX dramatically softens the MT's stark assertion that God willed the Servant's suffering. Where the MT says 'the LORD [willed] to crush him,' the LXX says 'the Lord wishes to cleanse him from his blow.' This removes the direct divine agency in the Servant's affliction and reframes God's intention as therapeutic rather than punitive. The translator may have found the MT's stark theodicy—God as the agent of the Servant's suffering—theologically problematic and resolved it by reconstruing the verb and object. This represents theological sanitization of a morally difficult text.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Hebrew says God wanted to crush the Servant, which is harsh. The Greek version says God wanted to cleanse him from his wounds, making God's role seem more merciful and less disturbing.
scholarly_citations: Seeligmann, The Septuagint Version of Isaiah, pp. 52-54
Hanhart, Studien zur Septuaginta und zum hellenistischen Judentum, pp. 201-204
revisions.counter_arguments: The LXX may reflect a different Hebrew Vorlage with a variant verb form; alternatively, the translator may have understood the Hebrew differently without conscious theological revision.

dominant_strategy: Messianic heightening is the most prominent revision type across Isaiah's textual traditions, particularly in the Targum. The targumic tradents systematically identify ambiguous royal and servant figures with 'the Messiah' (משיחא), transforming potentially polyvalent oracles into explicit messianic prophecy. This reflects the emerging proto-rabbinic hermeneutic that read Isaiah through a messianic lens, consolidating diverse prophetic materials into a coherent eschatological narrative centered on a Davidic deliverer. The LXX exhibits a different but complementary strategy: eschatological sharpening that reorients prophetic oracles toward future fulfillment, often through Israel as a collective agent, rather than explicit messianic individualization.
overall_assessment: The textual traditions of Isaiah reveal divergent yet overlapping theological strategies shaped by their respective communities' hermeneutical priorities. The Targum's aggressive messianic heightening and proto-rabbinic glossing reflect a Palestinian Jewish context where prophetic texts were being systematically aligned with emerging rabbinic messianism and halakhic concerns. The LXX's eschatological sharpening and universalizing tendencies reflect a Hellenistic-diaspora context where Isaiah's oracles were reinterpreted to address questions of Israel's role among the nations and the timing of restoration. Both traditions exhibit anthropomorphism avoidance, but deploy it differently: the Targum through 'glory' and 'from before' circumlocutions, the LXX through syntactic and lexical softening. The cumulative effect is that no major textual tradition preserves Isaiah without significant theological mediation, revealing the book's status as a living, interpretively unstable text throughout the Second Temple and early rabbinic periods.
overall_plain: The different ancient versions of Isaiah show how Jewish communities adapted the prophetic text to address their own theological questions. The Aramaic version made prophecies about the Messiah much more explicit, while the Greek version emphasized future restoration and Israel's role among the nations. Both versions softened anthropomorphic descriptions of God. These changes show that Isaiah was not transmitted as a static text but was continually reinterpreted to speak to new situations.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Targumic Transformation of the Suffering Servant Tradition
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The Targum's radical restructuring of Isaiah 52:13-53:12 represents the most theologically consequential revision in the entire Isaiah tradition. By transferring suffering from the Servant-Messiah to Israel's enemies and emphasizing the Messiah's victorious, Temple-building role, the Targum created an interpretive blockade against suffering-Messiah christologies that would become central to Christian claims. This revision illuminates a critical juncture in Jewish-Christian divergence and demonstrates that the 'plain sense' of Isaiah 53 was already contested within Second Temple Judaism, not merely between Judaism and Christianity. The targumic reading is not apologetic or reactive but represents an independent Jewish eschatological trajectory.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: The most significant change is how the Aramaic version completely reinterprets the Suffering Servant passage to remove any idea that the Messiah himself would suffer. This shows that even within ancient Judaism, there were different ways of understanding this famous prophecy, long before Christian interpretation emerged.
